texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: China is very rich in lithium resources in salt lake brine. There are more than 80 salt lakes located in Tibetan Plateau, and the reserve of lithium resources in these lakes reaches more than 5 million tons [1,2]. Unfortunately, the majority of salt lake brine is characterized
texts.text: China is very rich in lithium resources in salt lake brine. There are more than 80 salt lakes located in Tibetan Plateau, and the reserve of lithium resources in these lakes reaches more than 5 million tons [1,2]. Unfortunately, the majority of salt lake brine is characterized

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Extraction of lithium from brine is becoming a focus of importance worldwide. Lithium was extracted from brine bymembraneelectrolysis in this paper, and the influences of operating parameters on Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode are investigated. Various parameters including initial lithium concentration of anolyte, anode -cathode distance, electrolyte temperature, surface density of active substrate and electrolysis time are optimized. Under the optimal conditions, the electrode exhibits a remarkable Li + exchange capacity of 38.9 mg/g and the pH value of anolyte is less than 8. The results are beneficial for the extraction of lithium from brine by membrane electrolysis.
texts.text: Extraction of lithium from brine is becoming a focus of importance worldwide. Lithium was extracted from brine bymembraneelectrolysis in this paper, and the influences of operating parameters on Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode are investigated. Various parameters including initial lithium concentration of anolyte, anode -cathode distance, electrolyte temperature, surface density of active substrate and electrolysis time are optimized. Under the optimal conditions, the electrode exhibits a remarkable Li + exchange capacity of 38.9 mg/g and the pH value of anolyte is less than 8. The results are beneficial for the extraction of lithium from brine by membrane electrolysis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: with the high mass ratio of Mg/Li. The mass ratio of Mg/Li in most salt lake brine exceeds 40:1, and the highest even reaches an astonishing numerical value of 1837:1 [3 -5]. Due to the similar chemical properties of Li + and Mg 2+ , it's very difficult to separate Li from Mg in brine. Plenty of work has been done by many researchers, and many methods such as precipitation [6], solvent extraction [7,8], nanofiltration [9,10] and adsorption [11] have been developed to extract lithium from brine, but no effective method can be used to extract lithium effectively from high Mg/Li ratio salt lake brine.
texts.text: with the high mass ratio of Mg/Li. The mass ratio of Mg/Li in most salt lake brine exceeds 40:1, and the highest even reaches an astonishing numerical value of 1837:1 [3 -5]. Due to the similar chemical properties of Li + and Mg 2+ , it's very difficult to separate Li from Mg in brine. Plenty of work has been done by many researchers, and many methods such as precipitation [6], solvent extraction [7,8], nanofiltration [9,10] and adsorption [11] have been developed to extract lithium from brine, but no effective method can be used to extract lithium effectively from high Mg/Li ratio salt lake brine.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recently, membrane electrolysis is widely used in the separation process, such as wastewater treatment and reclamation facilities [12 -14]. In our research, a novel membrane electrolysis method was developed to extract lithium from high Mg/Li ratio salt lake brine [15,16]. It is well known that LiFePO4 is recognized as a promising cathode material for lithium ion battery [17 -19]. The reaction process is shown in the following formulas:
texts.text: Recently, membrane electrolysis is widely used in the separation process, such as wastewater treatment and reclamation facilities [12 -14]. In our research, a novel membrane electrolysis method was developed to extract lithium from high Mg/Li ratio salt lake brine [15,16]. It is well known that LiFePO4 is recognized as a promising cathode material for lithium ion battery [17 -19]. The reaction process is shown in the following formulas:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Charge process : LiFePO4 → Li þ þ FePO4 þ e ð 1 Þ

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Discharge process : FePO4 þ Li þ þ e → LiFePO4 : ð 2 Þ

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Unlike the traditional application, LiFePO4 and FePO4 are used to extract lithium from brine in this work. As shown in Fig. 1, the electrolytic cell is divided into two slots by anion exchange membrane. One slot is filled with salt lake brine and the other with Mg 2+ -free electrolyte. FePO4 anode is placed into the brine and LiFePO4 cathode into the Mg 2+ -free electrolyte. Driven by the external potential, Li + in the
texts.text: Unlike the traditional application, LiFePO4 and FePO4 are used to extract lithium from brine in this work. As shown in Fig. 1, the electrolytic cell is divided into two slots by anion exchange membrane. One slot is filled with salt lake brine and the other with Mg 2+ -free electrolyte. FePO4 anode is placed into the brine and LiFePO4 cathode into the Mg 2+ -free electrolyte. Driven by the external potential, Li + in the

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 2. Effect of Li + initial concentration of anolyte on exchange capacity of electrode.
texts.text: Fig. 2. Effect of Li + initial concentration of anolyte on exchange capacity of electrode.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 3. Effect of Li + initial concentration of anolyte on pH of electrolyte.
texts.text: Fig. 3. Effect of Li + initial concentration of anolyte on pH of electrolyte.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: brine is intercalated into FePO4 to produce LiFePO4, and Li + is released into the Mg 2+ -free electrolyte from LiFePO4 cathode to produce FePO4 in the membrane electrolysis process. In other words, FePO4 in brine reacts with Li + to produce LiFePO4, and LiFePO4 in Mg 2+ -free electrolyte is decomposed into Li + and FePO4. At the same time, the anions transfer from the brine to the Mg 2+ -free electrolyte to maintain the charge balance because the cations cannot penetrate the anion exchange membrane. By this way, Li + can be extracted selectively from salt lake brine into the Mg 2+ -free solution. The mass ratio of Mg/Li can be reduced to 0.3 from 493 by this method [20], indicating that it is efficient to separate magnesium and lithium from salt lake brine. Moreover, the effects of Na + , K + and Mg 2+ on Li extraction were investigated in our previous work and the side-effects of Na + , K + and Mg 2+ can be eliminated by controlling proper electrolytic voltage [15,20]. However, the Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode, which is critical to meet the requirement of electrode cycling, has not been taken into account in previous work. If the Li + exchange capacity of electrode is higher, the needed weight of electrode will be reduced, which is beneficial for the decrease of cost. For the meantime, the stability of electrode is critical to the cycle times of electrode used in the membrane electrolysis process. In our previous research work, the results showed that the FePO4 electrode will decompose if the pH of anolyte is more than 8 [21]. In that case, Li + in the solution cannot intercalate into FePO4 electrode to form LiFePO4. Finally, lithium in brine cannot be extracted by membrane electrolysis. Therefore, how to enhance the Li + exchange capacity of electrode and maintain the pH of solution in reasonable region are very important for the application of this novel method. Therefore, based on the previous research, the influence of other factors on Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode during the membrane electrolysis was studied in this paper.
texts.text: brine is intercalated into FePO4 to produce LiFePO4, and Li + is released into the Mg 2+ -free electrolyte from LiFePO4 cathode to produce FePO4 in the membrane electrolysis process. In other words, FePO4 in brine reacts with Li + to produce LiFePO4, and LiFePO4 in Mg 2+ -free electrolyte is decomposed into Li + and FePO4. At the same time, the anions transfer from the brine to the Mg 2+ -free electrolyte to maintain the charge balance because the cations cannot penetrate the anion exchange membrane. By this way, Li + can be extracted selectively from salt lake brine into the Mg 2+ -free solution. The mass ratio of Mg/Li can be reduced to 0.3 from 493 by this method [20], indicating that it is efficient to separate magnesium and lithium from salt lake brine. Moreover, the effects of Na + , K + and Mg 2+ on Li extraction were investigated in our previous work and the side-effects of Na + , K + and Mg 2+ can be eliminated by controlling proper electrolytic voltage [15,20]. However, the Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode, which is critical to meet the requirement of electrode cycling, has not been taken into account in previous work. If the Li + exchange capacity of electrode is higher, the needed weight of electrode will be reduced, which is beneficial for the decrease of cost. For the meantime, the stability of electrode is critical to the cycle times of electrode used in the membrane electrolysis process. In our previous research work, the results showed that the FePO4 electrode will decompose if the pH of anolyte is more than 8 [21]. In that case, Li + in the solution cannot intercalate into FePO4 electrode to form LiFePO4. Finally, lithium in brine cannot be extracted by membrane electrolysis. Therefore, how to enhance the Li + exchange capacity of electrode and maintain the pH of solution in reasonable region are very important for the application of this novel method. Therefore, based on the previous research, the influence of other factors on Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode during the membrane electrolysis was studied in this paper.

texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Electrode preparation
texts.text: 2.1. Electrode preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The LiFePO4 electrode was obtained in the following procedure: 90 wt.% LiFePO4 (active substrate), 5 wt.% acetylene black and 5 wt.% polyvinylidence fluoride(PVDF) binder were well mixed and then dispersed completely in N-methy1-2 Pyrolidone(NMP) to produce slurry. The slurry was overlaid onto the graphite plate with the size of 4 × 4 cm 2 according to predesigned surface density and then dried in vacuum at 110 °C for 10 h.
texts.text: The LiFePO4 electrode was obtained in the following procedure: 90 wt.% LiFePO4 (active substrate), 5 wt.% acetylene black and 5 wt.% polyvinylidence fluoride(PVDF) binder were well mixed and then dispersed completely in N-methy1-2 Pyrolidone(NMP) to produce slurry. The slurry was overlaid onto the graphite plate with the size of 4 × 4 cm 2 according to predesigned surface density and then dried in vacuum at 110 °C for 10 h.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: FePO4 anode was obtained by deintercalating lithium from LiFePO4 cathode. The electrolytic cell was filled with 1 mol · L -1 NaCl solution, and then LiFePO4 cathode and nickel anode were put into the electrolytic cell. A constant electrolysis of 1.0 V was ended until the ultimate

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Experimental procedure
texts.text: 2.2. Experimental procedure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane electrolysis experiments were carried out in the electrolytic cell as shown in Fig. 1. LiFePO4 cathode and FePO4 anode were placed into the electrolytic cell. The anion exchange membrane (MA-7500) was produced by SYBRON. 1 mol · L -1 NaCl solution was added to the cathode slot, and 1 mol · L -1 NaCl and predetermined Li + concentration mixed solution was added into anode slot. With unchanged voltage of 0.25 V, the membrane electrolysis process was carried out under predesigned conditions.
texts.text: The membrane electrolysis experiments were carried out in the electrolytic cell as shown in Fig. 1. LiFePO4 cathode and FePO4 anode were placed into the electrolytic cell. The anion exchange membrane (MA-7500) was produced by SYBRON. 1 mol · L -1 NaCl solution was added to the cathode slot, and 1 mol · L -1 NaCl and predetermined Li + concentration mixed solution was added into anode slot. With unchanged voltage of 0.25 V, the membrane electrolysis process was carried out under predesigned conditions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Analysis
texts.text: 2.3. Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Li + exchange capacity of electrode can be calculated by measuring the change of concentration of Li + in electrolyte. The concentration of Li + was analyzed by an atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS, Persee of Beijing, China). Lithium lamp is used and the wave length is 670.8 nm during the Li + concentration test process.
texts.text: The Li + exchange capacity of electrode can be calculated by measuring the change of concentration of Li + in electrolyte. The concentration of Li + was analyzed by an atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS, Persee of Beijing, China). Lithium lamp is used and the wave length is 670.8 nm during the Li + concentration test process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Results and discussion
texts.text: 3. Results and discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Effect of initial lithium concentration on Li extraction process
texts.text: 3.1. Effect of initial lithium concentration on Li extraction process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of Li + initial concentration in anolyte on electrolytic process was investigated from 50 mg/L to 1000 mg/L. Experimental conditions of electrolysis are as follows: voltage 0.25 V, reaction time 900 min, anode -cathode-distance 2 cm, temperature 25 °C, and surface density of active substrate 18 mg/cm 2 . The results are shown in Figs. 2 and 3. It can be seen from Fig. 2 that the Li + exchange capacities of electrode increase obviously with the increase of Li + initial concentration in anolyte. Li + in the cathode is released into the catholyte during the electrolysis, and at the same time, Li + in anolyte is intercalated into the anode. The Li + concentration in anolyte decreases gradually, which results in a decrease of the electrolysis efficiency. As Li + initial concentration increases from 50 mg/L to 200 mg/L, the Li + deintercalated exchange capacities of cathode increases from 30.1 to 38.9 mg/g, which is 87.8% of theoretical capacity, and the Li + intercalated exchange capacities of anode increases from 25.1 to 37.4 mg/g. However, further increase of Li + concentration does not result in obvious increase of Li + exchange capacity of electrode when the Li + initial concentration is beyond 200 mg/L. Fig. 3 illustrates that the final pH values of electrolyte decrease with the increase of Li + initial concentration of anolyte. This is because the increase of Li + concentration is beneficial for the shift of Li + from anolyte to anode, which can weaken the anodic polarization andinhibit the electrochemical hydrogen evolution side reaction. According to the literature [21], it is harmful to the stability of FePO4 electrode in anolyte when the pH value is more than 8. Generally, Li + initial

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Effect of anode -cathode-distance on Li extraction process
texts.text: 3.2. Effect of anode -cathode-distance on Li extraction process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 exhibits the effect of anode -cathode distance on Li + exchange capacity of electrode. It can be seen that there is not obvious effect on Li + exchange capacity. The deintercalated exchange capacity of cathode after 900 min increases from 37 to 39.1 mg/g when the anode -cathode distance decreases from 18 to 2 cm, and the increase of anode intercalated exchange capacity is only 1.3 mg/g under this condition. This is because that the diffusion speed of Li + in electrode is far less than in electrolyte when the Li + concentration in anolyte is 500 mg/L. Therefore, the electrode exhibits similar Li + exchange capacity although the anode -cathode distance ranges from 2 to 18 cm. Fig. 5 shows that there is slow increase of pH value with the increase of anode -cathode-distance. The pH values of catholyte are all less than 7 under different anode -cathode-distance conditions, but the pH value of anolyte reaches 8.26 when the anode -cathode-distance is 18 cm, which is harmful to the stability of FePO4 electrode.
texts.text: Fig. 4 exhibits the effect of anode -cathode distance on Li + exchange capacity of electrode. It can be seen that there is not obvious effect on Li + exchange capacity. The deintercalated exchange capacity of cathode after 900 min increases from 37 to 39.1 mg/g when the anode -cathode distance decreases from 18 to 2 cm, and the increase of anode intercalated exchange capacity is only 1.3 mg/g under this condition. This is because that the diffusion speed of Li + in electrode is far less than in electrolyte when the Li + concentration in anolyte is 500 mg/L. Therefore, the electrode exhibits similar Li + exchange capacity although the anode -cathode distance ranges from 2 to 18 cm. Fig. 5 shows that there is slow increase of pH value with the increase of anode -cathode-distance. The pH values of catholyte are all less than 7 under different anode -cathode-distance conditions, but the pH value of anolyte reaches 8.26 when the anode -cathode-distance is 18 cm, which is harmful to the stability of FePO4 electrode.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Effect of membrane electrolysis temperature on Li extraction process
texts.text: 3.3. Effect of membrane electrolysis temperature on Li extraction process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to investigate the effect of temperature on Li + exchange capacity of electrode, membrane electrolysis experiments are performed from 25 to 60 °C, and the results are shown in Fig. 6. It can be seen
texts.text: In order to investigate the effect of temperature on Li + exchange capacity of electrode, membrane electrolysis experiments are performed from 25 to 60 °C, and the results are shown in Fig. 6. It can be seen

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 7. Current distribution curves at different surface density.
texts.text: Fig. 7. Current distribution curves at different surface density.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: that temperature has a significant effect on the migration speed of Li + at the early stage of reaction. After 540 min, the Li + deintercalated exchange capacity of cathode increases obviously from 32.1 to 38.3 mg/g when the temperature increases from 25 to 60 °C, and the anode shows the similar tendency due to the law of charge conservation. However, the ultimate capacities at different temperatures are close to each other after 900 min.
texts.text: that temperature has a significant effect on the migration speed of Li + at the early stage of reaction. After 540 min, the Li + deintercalated exchange capacity of cathode increases obviously from 32.1 to 38.3 mg/g when the temperature increases from 25 to 60 °C, and the anode shows the similar tendency due to the law of charge conservation. However, the ultimate capacities at different temperatures are close to each other after 900 min.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Effect of surface density of electrode on Li extraction process
texts.text: 3.4. Effect of surface density of electrode on Li extraction process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Surface density is relevant to the weight of the active substrate coated on unit area. To study the effect of surface density of active substrate, membrane electrolysis experiments are conducted on conditions of Li + initial concentration of anolyte 200 mg/L, anode -cathode distance 2 cm, temperature 25 °C and reaction time 900 min. As shown in Figs. 7 and 8, the cathode delivers a Li + deintercalated exchange capacity of 38.9 mg/g with a current plateau near 7.5 mA at a low surface density of 12 mg/cm 2 . When the surface density is increased to 18 mg/cm 2 , the cathode delivers a little decrease of Li + exchange capacity to 36.2 mg/g, and the current plateau still remains near 7.5 mA. When the surface density is further increased to 24 mg/cm 2 , the cathode exhibits an obvious decrease of Li + exchange capacity to 27.5 mg/g, meanwhile, the current plateau increases to about 9 mA. The increase of surface density is beneficial to enhance the space efficiency of the electrode, but it is adverse to the transport of Li + in electrode. The thinner is the electrode, the easier Li + moves, and the higher is the Li + exchange capacity of electrode. Therefore, the increase of surface density leads to the decrease of Li + exchange capacity of electrode unavoidably, and the cathode and anode show
texts.text: Surface density is relevant to the weight of the active substrate coated on unit area. When the surface density is further increased to 24 mg/cm 2 , the cathode exhibits an obvious decrease of Li + exchange capacity to 27.5 mg/g, meanwhile, the current plateau increases to about 9 mA. The increase of surface density is beneficial to enhance the space efficiency of the electrode, but it is adverse to the transport of Li + in electrode. The thinner is the electrode, the easier Li + moves, and the higher is the Li + exchange capacity of electrode. texts.label: text
texts.orig: similar tendency. When the surface density is up to 28 mg/cm 2 , the Li + exchange capacity of cathode is only 23.5 mg/g, and Li + exchange capacity of anode is only 17 mg/g. The increase of surface density not only makes Li + move more slowly, but also leads to the occurrence of side-reaction such as hydrogen evolution reaction due to the deterioration of electrode polarization.
texts.text: similar tendency. When the surface density is up to 28 mg/cm 2 , the Li + exchange capacity of cathode is only 23.5 mg/g, and Li + exchange capacity of anode is only 17 mg/g. The increase of surface density not only makes Li + move more slowly, but also leads to the occurrence of side-reaction such as hydrogen evolution reaction due to the deterioration of electrode polarization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The comparison shown in Fig. 9 clearly demonstrates that the increase of surface density brings about the increase of the pH value of electrolyte, especially the anolyte. The anolyte has a pH of 8.7 when the surface density of active substrate is 28 mg/cm 2 , which results in the dissolution of anode in anolyte. Therefore, surface density of 18mg/cm 2 is considered to be optimal for both the efficiency and stability reasons.
texts.text: The comparison shown in Fig. 9 clearly demonstrates that the increase of surface density brings about the increase of the pH value of electrolyte, especially the anolyte. The anolyte has a pH of 8.7 when the surface density of active substrate is 28 mg/cm 2 , which results in the dissolution of anode in anolyte. Therefore, surface density of 18mg/cm 2 is considered to be optimal for both the efficiency and stability reasons.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.5. Effect of membrane electrolysis time on Li extraction process
texts.text: 3.5. Effect of membrane electrolysis time on Li extraction process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The effect of electrolysis time on electrolytic process was studied from 0 to 1080 min under the conditions of Li + initial concentration of anolyte 200 mg/L, anode -cathode distance 2 cm, temperature 25 °C and surface density of active substrate 18 mg/cm 2 , and the results are shown in Figs. 10 and 11. As shown in Fig. 10, the electrolysis time has a remarkable effect on Li + exchange capacity of electrode. As the time increases from 60 to 540 min, the Li + exchange capacity of cathode raises from 6.4 to 32.1 mg/g, and the Li + exchange capacities of electrode increase slowly when the time is over 900 min. Nevertheless, the change of pH value shows a different trend. The pH value of catholyte shows little increase during the whole process, and no obvious change can be observed when the time is over 360 min. However,
texts.text: The effect of electrolysis time on electrolytic process was studied from 0 to 1080 min under the conditions of Li + initial concentration of anolyte 200 mg/L, anode -cathode distance 2 cm, temperature 25 °C and surface density of active substrate 18 mg/cm 2 , and the results are shown in Figs. 10 and 11. As shown in Fig. 10, the electrolysis time has a remarkable effect on Li + exchange capacity of electrode. As the time increases from 60 to 540 min, the Li + exchange capacity of cathode raises from 6.4 to 32.1 mg/g, and the Li + exchange capacities of electrode increase slowly when the time is over 900 min. Nevertheless, the change of pH value shows a different trend. The pH value of catholyte shows little increase during the whole process, and no obvious change can be observed when the time is over 360 min. However,

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode during membrane electrolysis under different conditions has been studied in this work. The results indicate that the initial lithium concentration of anolyte, surface density of active substrate and membrane electrolysis time will affect the Li + exchange capacity of electrode significantly, and the low lithium concentration of anolyte, high surface density of active substrate and long membrane electrolysis time will cause the increase of the pH value of anolyte. The optimum operating parameters for membrane electrolysis were established as follows: initial lithium concentration of anolyte 200 mg/L, anode -cathode distance 2 cm, electrolyte temperature 25 °C, surface density of active substrate 18 mg/cm 2 and electrolysis time 900 min. Under such conditions, the electrode exhibits a remarkable Li + exchange capacity of 38.9 mg/g and the pH value of anolyte is less than 8, which is beneficial for the stability of electrode in electrolyte during the membrane electrolysis process.
texts.text: The Li + exchange capacity and stability of electrode during membrane electrolysis under different conditions has been studied in this work. The results indicate that the initial lithium concentration of anolyte, surface density of active substrate and membrane electrolysis time will affect the Li + exchange capacity of electrode significantly, and the low lithium concentration of anolyte, high surface density of active substrate and long membrane electrolysis time will cause the increase of the pH value of anolyte. The optimum operating parameters for membrane electrolysis were established as follows: initial lithium concentration of anolyte 200 mg/L, anode -cathode distance 2 cm, electrolyte temperature 25 °C, surface density of active substrate 18 mg/cm 2 and electrolysis time 900 min. Under such conditions, the electrode exhibits a remarkable Li + exchange capacity of 38.9 mg/g and the pH value of anolyte is less than 8, which is beneficial for the stability of electrode in electrolyte during the membrane electrolysis process.
